--- title: "Hip-Hop Periodic Table" subtitle: "A Data Storytelling Teaching Module" author: "Data Storytelling in Professional Practice" format: dashboard: theme: - cosmo - ../custom.scss logo: "" nav-buttons: - icon: github href: "https://github.com/lwylie01/my-site" scrolling: true execute: echo: false warning: false message: false --- ::: {.cell} ::: # Overview {.sidebar} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Dataset at a Glance

132 artists across 6 eras

6 geographic regions

7 lyrical styles

Confidence breakdown

H: 67 artists
M: 53 artists (some gaps)
L: 12 artists (estimates only)

Mean composite score

6.96 / 10

Highest: Kendrick Lamar

Teaching concepts
  • Measurement validity
  • Sampling & bias
  • Multidimensional scoring
  • Composite vs. dimensions
  • Uncertainty communication
  • Visualization design
``` ::: ::: # 1 · What Are We Measuring? ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-tip title="Teaching Prompt: Construct Validity"} **The central measurement problem:** We started with one variable called "Lyricism" and ended up with five. Was that the right decision? What are we gaining and losing? This is the construct validity question that underlies every performance measure, survey instrument, and rating scale in your organization. ::: ## Row {height="85%"} ### Radar: One artist, five dimensions {width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Scatter: Composite score vs. Storytelling {width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 2 · Who Is In the Data? ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-warning title="Teaching Prompt: Sampling Decisions and Bias"} **Every dataset has a frame, and every frame has a gap.** This dataset over-represents critically acclaimed artists, NYC, the Golden Age, and men. Those are documented choices. What datasets do you use at work where nobody wrote the frame down? Who is missing? ::: ## Row {height="45%"} ### Artists by region {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender representation gap {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Row {height="40%"} ### Confidence distribution {width="35%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### What bias looks like in practice {width="65%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 3 · Dimensions vs. Composite ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-note title="Teaching Prompt: The Averaging Problem"} **What does averaging destroy?** Rakim (Rhyme Density 10, composite 8.8) and Scarface (Storytelling 10, composite 8.2) land within half a point of each other on the composite, yet their profiles look nothing alike. Does that similarity mean anything, or does the average erase the most important information? When does averaging help, and when does it mislead? ::: ## Row {height="45%"} ### Heatmap: All artists, all dimensions {width="65%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Era averages by dimension {width="35%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Row {height="40%"} ### Strongest dimension by style {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Top 20 composite scores (High confidence) {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 4 · Uncertainty in Practice ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-important title="Teaching Prompt: Communicating Uncertainty"} **Most dashboards show you the number. This one shows you how much to trust it.** The confidence flag almost never shows up in stakeholder reporting, but it should. Where in your organization's reporting does uncertainty get hidden? What decisions are being made on L-confidence data presented as H-confidence? ::: ## Row {height="45%"} ### Composite distribution by confidence tier {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Score range: What we don't know {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Row {height="40%"} ### Confidence notes for M and L entries {width="100%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 5 · Visualization Design ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-tip title="Teaching Prompt: The Same Data, Different Stories"} **Every visualization choice is an argument.** The four charts below show the same data (composite scores by era) drawn four different ways. Each one highlights something and hides something else. Which is most honest? Which is most persuasive? Are those the same chart? ::: ## Row {height="85%"} ### A: Ranked bar {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### B: Time trend line {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### C: Distribution (violin) {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### D: Scatter with n shown {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 6 · Full Data Table ## Row ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
